OpenAI acquires Astral to bolster its Codex programming assistant
data.description:
This acquisition comes at a time of rapid adoption for AI-driven programming assistants. OpenAI reports that Codex now has over 2 million weekly active users, with its user base tripling since the beginning of the year. The company is seeking to solidify its position against rivals such as Anthropic and Cursor.

The deal remains subject to customary regulatory approvals and is part of a broader acquisition strategy. OpenAI has recently ramped up its buyouts, including the $6.4bn acquisition of startup io, as well as Promptfoo in cybersecurity and Torch in healthcare. The hiring of former Google executive Albert Lee to lead the external development strategy confirms this expansionist drive.

data.title: Trump: I expect Japan to step up
Micron buoyed by explosive demand, despite market jitters
data.description:
The memory chip specialist remains at the heart of the current artificial intelligence momentum, driven by sustained demand for DRAM and NAND, as well as the arrival of next-generation memory such as HBM4. In this context, Micron reported adjusted EPS of $12.20, well above the $9.31 expected. Revenue reached $23.86bn, above the consensus of $20.07bn. Year-on-year, revenues nearly tripled from $8.05bn in the same period last year.

Outlook remains highly favorable

The group anticipates this momentum will continue, with revenue of approximately $33.5bn for the current quarter, representing an increase of more than 200% y-o-y. Adjusted EPS is expected at $19.15, again significantly above market forecasts.

Net income also rose sharply, reaching $13.8bn compared to $1.58bn a year earlier. To support this growth, Micron announced a $5bn increase in capex to expand production capacity. This strategy mirrors those of its competitors, notably Samsung and SK Hynix, amid persistent supply constraints.

Short-term concerns over investment levels

However, this increase in investment comes amid growing questions regarding the AI sector's ability to generate sustainable profits. These announcements cooled investor sentiment, leading to a mid-session decline in the share price.

Despite this short-term reaction, several analysts remain confident in Micron's prospects. Vijay Rakesh, an analyst at Mizuho Securities, raised his estimates and maintained his "outperform" rating, with a price target increased to $530. He believes the group benefits from a solid positioning, with strong profitability and robust roadmaps in the DRAM and NAND segments.

Meanwhile, Harlan Sur, a hardware specialist at JP Morgan, sought to downplay fears of a potential slowdown in margin growth. According to him, "the key point remains the sustainability of these margin levels: the structural supply constraints supporting current prices are not expected to be resolved by the arrival of new capacity before the end of 2028."

Freeport launches $7.5bn expansion project in Chile
data.description:
The company indicated that the permitting procedures are expected to begin between late 2025 and early 2026. The project notably includes the construction of a concentrator plant as well as a desalination unit, designed to secure water supply in an arid region. This infrastructure is intended to significantly increase the site's production capacity.

The new facilities are expected to come online within the next decade. This development comes amid rising demand for copper, a metal essential to the energy transition and electrical technologies, reinforcing the interest of major mining groups in expanding their capacities.

Freeport-McMoRan shares are tumbling 6.5% in early trading on Wall Street.

Iran war escalation wakes markets up to risks of deeper economic pain
data.description:
LONDON, March 19 (Reuters) - Investors reassessing the potential economic fallout from the war in Iran are selling assets across the globe, from government bonds to stocks and gold, reigniting fears that markets may become vulnerable to a bigger dislocation.

Oil prices jumped to as high as $119 a barrel on Thursday as Iran attacked energy facilities across the Middle East following Israel's strike on its South Pars gas field.

European gas prices surged 22% in just one day, highlighting the region's energy dependency.

The pain was felt globally and exacerbated by hawkish signalling from central banks including the U.S. Federal Reserve, with all G7 central banks meeting within less than 24 hours in a rare coincidence.

Traders, growing more worried about inflation risks, are no longer confident the Fed will cut rates this year and boosted the rate hike bets they've put on across Europe's central banks, which they expect to be more responsive to higher energy prices after a 2022 energy crisis sent inflation soaring.

These worries sent government bond yields from Britain to Italy and the United States surging again on Thursday.

The pain was most stark in the UK, where two-year yields, sensitive to interest rate expectations, jumped over 30 basis points (bps). They were set for their biggest daily increase since former Prime Minister Liz Truss's failed 2022 economic plan.

In a sign that investors, who analysts say have priced in a relatively short-lived conflict so far, are growing more concerned, gold fell 4%. European stocks were set for their second biggest daily fall since the conflict broke out.

Even the dollar, a rare winner from the conflict, dropped against peers on Thursday, falling 1% against the yen and 0.6% against the euro.

"For the first time that bought energy infrastructure into the conflict," Lloyds currency strategist Nick Kennedy said, referring to the latest attacks.

"That is a clear escalation and you don't know where that ends up, so markets are right to be a bit more cautious, as it has crossed the Rubicon."

RATE HIKE BETS

The Bank of England vindicated traders' hawkish bets on Thursday, when policymakers voted unanimously to keep rates on hold, and some raised the prospect of raising rates.

Traders now price in two BoE rate hikes by year-end, having expected it would cut rates at this meeting before the war. At one point they priced in a high chance of a third move before governor Andrew Bailey pushed back on market pricing.

At the ECB, which also met on Thursday, traders fully price in two rate hikes and a strong chance of a third by December.

Euro area and U.S. short-dated bond yields surged about 10 bps.

The hawkish repricing first gained momentum following Wednesday's Fed meeting.

(Reporting by Yoruk Bahceli and Samuel Indyk; additional reporting by Dhara Ranasinghe ; Editing by Dhara Ranasinghe)

By Yoruk Bahceli and Samuel Indyk
